name: Hazel Green
short_description: Hunt for quartz, calcite, and feldspar specimens in Grant County's historic lead and zinc mining region. Public access available with reasonable collecting limits.
description:
Hazel Green sits in southwestern Wisconsin within the historic Upper Mississippi Valley lead-zinc district, one of America's most productive mineral regions of the 19th and early 20th centuries. The area is rich in Paleozoic carbonate bedrock (Galena and Platteville Dolomite formations) that hosted the lead and zinc ore deposits which drove regional settlement and commerce. Today, rockhounds can explore roadcuts, quarries, and stream contexts where quartz veins, calcite crystals, and secondary minerals remain accessible to patient collectors.

The geology here reflects shallow Ordovician seas that deposited limestone and dolomite, later enriched with mineralization from hydrothermal circulation. Collectors will find white to clear quartz in various forms, from blocky crystals to massive veins. Calcite specimens range from transparent scalenohedral crystals to massive white and amber varieties. Feldspar, mica, and trace amounts of secondary copper minerals can also turn up with careful searching.

This region appeals to beginners and experienced rockhounders alike because specimens are accessible without special equipment or permits on most public and private lands (with permission). The landscape is rolling to gently hilly, with mixed deciduous forest and agricultural land, making for pleasant collecting conditions when weather permits. Hazel Green itself is a small village with basic services; nearby Platteville offers more amenities.

The best collecting happens in roadcuts along county roads and in the beds of seasonal streams draining the mineralized zones. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable conditions, though winter ice and muddy spring runoff can limit access. Most finds are specimen-grade rather than gem-quality, but the mineral variety and accessibility make this a worthwhile destination for Wisconsin rockhounders.
history: The Hazel Green area sits within the Galena-Platteville mining district, where lead and zinc ore was extensively mined from the 1820s through the 1980s. Ordovician-age dolomite and limestone, deposited 450+ million years ago in a shallow sea, hosted mineral-bearing hydrothermal fluids during Paleozoic structural activity, creating the quartz veins, calcite seams, and secondary mineral coatings that persist in roadcuts and mine tailings today.
tips: Look in freshly exposed roadcuts along country roads running through dolomite outcrop country, where weather and road maintenance have recently broken open the bedrock. Bring a hand lens to identify calcite's distinctive rhombohedral cleavage and quartz's vitreous luster; both stand out against the dolomite host rock. Check the beds and banks of spring-fed streams after rain when water erosion exposes fresh mineral specimens washed from upslope soils and weathered veins. Carry a small hammer and chisel to carefully extract crystals from matrix, and collect only what you can carry and reasonably identify.
terrain_notes: Gently rolling terrain at elevation 900-1100 feet, with mixed hardwood forest, open agricultural land, and scattered rural residences. Most productive areas are along roadcuts and stream valleys where bedrock is naturally exposed. Walking distances from parking are typically short (under half mile) to moderate (up to 1-2 miles for stream exploration). Footing ranges from firm grass and forest duff to rocky stream gravel and slippery muddy banks depending on recent weather.
best_season: May - September offers dry roads, comfortable temperatures, and accessible stream banks. Fall (September - October) is also excellent with cool weather and lower humidity. Winter ice and mud make winter and early spring challenging for casual rockhounders.
written_directions: From downtown Hazel Green, take WI-23 east toward Platteville for about 2 miles. Turn north on County Road H and continue for 3-4 miles through rolling farmland and forest; watch for dolomite roadcuts on both sides. Park safely in wide spots or farm access roads where road maintenance has exposed fresh bedrock. Smaller productive collecting areas lie on various county roads throughout Grant County within a 3-5 mile radius of Hazel Green village; consult a county map and always ask landowner permission before entering private property.
road_conditions: County roads are typically well-maintained gravel or paved surfaces; some rough patches and washboard are common after rain or snow melt. High-clearance vehicles recommended during wet periods.
parking_notes: Informal roadside parking in wide shoulders and farm access areas. No designated lots; park safely away from active traffic. Be respectful of private property boundaries.
collection_rules: Collecting is permitted on public roadways and public lands. Always seek permission before collecting on private property. Respect mine closures and posted restrictions. Do not trespass on active mining sites or quarries. Remove only what you can legally carry. Leave the area as clean as you found it.
rules:
Collect only on public land or with explicit private landowner permission
Do not enter posted or fenced areas
Do not remove specimens from active quarries or mines
Respect all environmental and trespass laws
Leave no trash; pack out all waste
nearest_services: Hazel Green village (1 mile) has limited services. Platteville, WI (8 miles east) offers gas, groceries, restaurants, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Most productive roadcuts are accessible from parked vehicles with minimal walking. Terrain is generally flat to gently rolling with firm footing on grass and road shoulders. Stream collecting requires walking on uneven gravel and muddy banks; some scrambling over rocks may be necessary. No wheelchair access to best collecting areas. Sturdy footwear and caution on slippery banks recommended.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can I find rockhounding near Hazel Green, Wisconsin?
faq.answer: The primary finds at Hazel Green are quartz (clear to milky white crystals), calcite (transparent to amber scalenohedral crystals), feldspar, and mica. Secondary copper minerals such as malachite can occasionally appear in trace amounts. Most specimens are collector-grade rather than gem-quality, but the mineral variety and accessibility make this an excellent site for Wisconsin rockhounders building mineral collections.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Hazel Green free, or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Hazel Green is free when collecting on public roadways and publicly accessible land. No permits or fees are required. However, always seek explicit permission from private landowners before collecting on their property, and strictly respect mine closures and posted restrictions.

faq.question: When is the best time of year to go rockhounding in Hazel Green?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Hazel Green runs from May through September, when roads are dry, weather is comfortable, and stream banks are easily accessible. Fall (September - October) is also excellent with cool temperatures and low humidity. Winter ice, spring mud, and unpredictable weather make other seasons less appealing for casual collectors.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to collect minerals at Hazel Green?
faq.answer: A small hammer and chisel are essential for carefully extracting crystals from matrix rock. Bring a hand lens or loupe to identify specimens in the field using cleavage patterns and luster. A sturdy backpack, gloves, and closed-toe hiking boots are recommended. Roadcut collecting requires minimal equipment; stream prospecting benefits from a small net to sift gravel.

faq.question: Is Hazel Green rockhounding suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Hazel Green is very beginner and family-friendly. Most productive roadcuts are easily accessible from parked vehicles, and specimens are large enough for inexperienced collectors to identify and extract. The rolling terrain and short walking distances make it suitable for children ages 6 and up. Clear identification guides for quartz, calcite, and feldspar help kids learn mineral properties quickly.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog while rockhounding at Hazel Green?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on public lands and roadways around Hazel Green. Keep your dog under control, stay away from active farm areas and wildlife habitat, and pack out all waste. The open terrain and moderate walking distances make this a dog-friendly rockhounding destination in Wisconsin.

meta_description: Find quartz, calcite, and feldspar at Hazel Green, Wisconsin. Free public rockhounding in the historic Upper Mississippi lead-zinc district.
